A visual self-assessment or peer-assessment tool that allows for the evaluation of different criteria on a scale represented as a target. The closer to the center, the greater the degree of achievement or agreement.

## How it Works
1. The teacher defines the criteria to be assessed.

2. Each criterion occupies a sector of the target.

3. Students mark their level of achievement in each sector.

4. Perceptions are compared, marks are justified, and improvements are proposed.

5. It can be repeated at the beginning and end of a task to track progress.

## Objectives
- To facilitate a quick and visual evaluation.

- To promote reflection on several criteria simultaneously.

- To help formulate proposals for improvement.

## Example
After an oral presentation, each student evaluates clarity, mastery of the content, use of visual aids, and team participation. They then write a specific action for improvement.

name: Learning Journal
summary: This tool allows students to regularly record their learning experiences, difficulties, and progress. It promotes metacognition, work tracking, self-assessment, and awareness of their own learning process.
desc:
A learning journal is a tool where students periodically record their experiences in the learning process. While it's most common for it to be some type of text document, there's no problem with it being audio (podcast) or video, as long as the timeframe for its creation is respected.

In this type of journal, students express the difficulties they encounter, what they like or dislike, problems they might have with classmates, etc.

You can suggest the following:

| Some questions to reflect on in the journal |

---|

**Questions you should always include**:

🤔 What have we done this week?

😓 Areas where I'm having the most difficulty.|

**More suggestions**:

Areas where I find things easier.

Aspects I had pending that I've been able to resolve since last week.

What doubts I have and what I can do to resolve them.

Aspects I liked more/less.

I have helped my classmates, or they have helped me.

Am I keeping up with my work?

…|

They can write it once a week, dedicating about 10-15 minutes to it.

Source: De Haro, J.J. (2022). Tools for the evaluation of individual learning logs and teamwork.

## Difficulties in completing the learning log

There are different obstacles that students may encounter when completing the learning log:

- Lack of time: completing the learning log may require some time each week, and students may find themselves under pressure from other commitments such as homework, extracurricular activities, etc. To overcome this obstacle, a routine can be established for completing the learning log each week, and consistency is key. Alternatively, a time of the week can be chosen when there is more free time to do it.

- Lack of motivation: Perhaps students don't see the usefulness of a learning journal and, therefore, aren't motivated to keep one. In this case, you can talk to the students about the benefits of a learning journal, such as the opportunity to reflect on their own learning, identify difficulties, and find solutions, etc. You can also monitor their learning journal entries with them to help them see the progress they are making.

- Difficulty expressing themselves: Perhaps students have difficulty expressing their ideas and reflections in writing. In this case, you can offer support to improve their writing skills, such as writing exercises or providing examples of learning journal entries. You can also consider the option of completing the learning journal orally or via video instead of in writing.

## Example of an entry

"This week we studied the unit on forces in Physics. At first, I found it a little difficult to understand the concept of force, but I finally grasped it thanks to my teacher's explanation and the exercises we did in class." I also had difficulty solving the homework problems, but I asked my classmates and the teacher for help, and that's how I was able to solve them.

Of all the concepts in the unit, the practical problems were the most challenging. On the other hand, I understood the theoretical concepts very well. Even so, I still have some questions about how to apply friction in certain situations.

One of the things I enjoyed most this week was working as a team with my classmates to solve the problems. I also helped my classmates understand the unit's theoretical concepts.

I completed all the homework and reviewed the unit's concepts every day to be prepared for the assessments. I'm keeping up with my work.

name: Digital Storytelling
summary: This activity involves students creating digital stories by combining narration, images, audio, or video. It serves to communicate learning, develop creativity, organize information, and produce multimedia materials with expressive or explanatory intent.
desc:
Digital storytelling involves combining narratives and digital content, including images, sound, and video, to create a short film, usually with strong emotional content (Educause, 2011). Seven Essential Elements of Digital Storytelling.

The planning and development of multimedia projects in the form of a story (digital storytelling, DST) seeks to leverage new digital technologies to enhance this educational resource. Originally, DST focused primarily on narratives of personal experiences in video format, with voiceovers or interviews, lasting less than 5 minutes. Currently, digital storytelling can also integrate narrative and informational resources, typical of a more documentary-style genre.

Digital stories can be created by teachers, but with appropriate guidance, students can also create them (for more details, see 8 Steps to Implement Storytelling in the Classroom).

---

Source: Digital Storytelling in De Haro, J.J. (2022). Open ICT Tools and Services for Education.

## Example

In a biology unit on infectious diseases, students create a three-minute digital story titled “The Journey of a Virus”. Each team chooses a point of view: an infected cell, a sick person, the immune system or a medical team.

The final product combines a short narration, images, simple diagrams, music or sound effects and a scientific conclusion. The teacher asks the story to include concepts such as transmission, immune response, prevention and treatment. When presenting it, each group explains the narrative choices they made and the scientific content they wanted to communicate.

name: Roundtable
summary: A cooperative structure in which a sheet of paper circulates among the team and each member adds a contribution. It serves to activate prior knowledge, generate ideas, build collective responses, and encourage everyone's participation.
desc:
TEAM SIZE: Base team.

OBJECTIVES: To activate prior knowledge, generate new ideas based on existing ones, and assess comprehension of content.

STEPS:

1. The teacher assigns a task to the base teams (a word list, writing a story, what they know about a specific topic to assess their prior knowledge, a sentence summarizing a key idea from a text they have read, etc.).

1. One student in the team begins writing their answer on a sheet of paper and then passes it to the next student, rotating clockwise.

1. The next student writes their answer, adding new information to enrich the work. When finished, they pass it to the next student.

1. The sheet of paper continues to rotate until everyone has participated in the task, and as many times as necessary, until time runs out.

OBSERVATIONS AND PRACTICAL TIPS:
Remember that while one team member is writing their part, the others must pay attention, help, and correct them. It is also possible to carry out the activity silently. This way, the teacher can explore each member's contribution to the group (using different colors when writing).

To make the activity dynamic, establish a quick rotation, perhaps even using a timer.

Ask students to each write with a different color; this way, their contributions can be easily distinguished.

A small whiteboard can be used instead of a sheet of paper. Introduce materials that add novelty to the activity.

Observe the contributions of each team member.

Source: Pérez, M. (2015). Cooperative Dynamics.

---

**Explanation of the technique:**

## Example

In a language lesson, the teacher asks each team to write a short story based on an image. The first student writes the beginning of the story for one minute and passes the sheet to the classmate on the right. The second student continues the narration, the third introduces a conflict and the fourth proposes an ending.

In a second round, each student revises or improves one aspect of the text: vocabulary, coherence, connectors or spelling. At the end, the team reads the story and comments on each member’s contribution. The technique ensures that everyone participates and makes it possible to build a shared product through successive contributions.

name: Word Game
summary: This technique involves students creating sentences from keywords and then organizing them into a diagram or map. It serves to review content, develop summaries, build shared meaning, and strengthen cooperation.
desc:
1. The teacher writes several key words on the board about the topic being studied or recently completed.
1. In each base team, students write a sentence using one of the key words, or express the idea behind that word. To make the sentences easy to handle, they write them on small pieces of paper.
1. Then, following a set order, each student shows the sentence to the team. The other members correct it, refine it and complete it. In this way, the sentence becomes a collective sentence from the whole team, not only from the student who wrote it first.
1. Next, the team arranges the sentences on the table according to a logical criterion, creating a summary outline or concept map of the topic.
1. Once the teacher approves the order, the sentences are numbered and copied neatly, usually in pairs, so that every team member has a copy.

Criteria to promote participation and interaction:

- The key words may be the same for all teams, or each base team may have a different list. The sentences or ideas built from the key words represent a synthesis of the topic studied.
- Teams may decide which word each member will use, or the teacher may assign them. Students who need more support should receive words that make it easier to write a sentence.
- In later uses of the structure, a different student can start the correction round each time.
- When pairs copy the sentences neatly, they can take turns: one writes while the other dictates and checks accuracy.

Adapted from Pujolàs, P. & Lago, J. R. (2011). The CA/AC program for learning cooperatively.

## Example
The teacher writes key words such as “communication”, “leadership”, “team” and “conflict resolution”. Each student writes a sentence with one word, the team revises it, and finally they organize the sentences into a shared outline or concept map.

name: 5E Instructional Model
summary: A teaching model based on the phases of engaging, exploring, explaining, elaborating, and evaluating. It serves to structure active learning sequences, activate prior knowledge, promote inquiry, apply concepts, and evaluate throughout the process.
desc:
## Description
An instructional model that organizes an active learning sequence into five phases. It is especially common in science and inquiry-based learning, but it can be applied to many subjects when the aim is to combine curiosity, exploration, explanation, application and assessment.

## Phases
1. **Engage**: spark interest, introduce an initial situation and activate prior knowledge.
2. **Explore**: investigate, observe, manipulate data or materials and develop initial explanations.
3. **Explain**: organize ideas, introduce formal concepts and build a shared explanation.
4. **Elaborate**: apply learning in new contexts, problems or transfer tasks.
5. **Evaluate**: collect evidence, review understanding and provide feedback during and at the end of the process.

## How it works
1. Design a sequence that starts with a motivating question, phenomenon or challenge.
2. Students explore before receiving the full formal explanation.
3. The teacher helps organize ideas and connect experience with concepts.
4. New situations are proposed to apply and transfer learning.
5. Assessment is integrated throughout the sequence.

## Objectives
- Structure active learning sequences.
- Activate prior knowledge and promote inquiry.
- Support application, transfer and formative assessment.

## Complete example
Topic: **What do seeds need to germinate?**

1. **Engage**: the teacher shows two pots, one with germinated seeds and another with seeds that have not germinated. The teacher asks: “If both pots had seeds, why did they germinate in one pot but not in the other?”. Students write their initial ideas about the role of water, light, temperature and soil type.
2. **Explore**: in teams, students design and carry out a small investigation. They place seeds under different conditions: with water and without water, with light and without light, in a cold place and at room temperature. They observe changes over several days and record data in a table.
3. **Explain**: each team shares its results. Based on the observations, the class builds an initial explanation. Then the teacher introduces the concepts of germination, necessary conditions, control of variables and experimental evidence.
4. **Elaborate**: students apply what they have learned to a new situation: designing recommendations for germinating seeds in a school garden or explaining why some seeds stored at home do not germinate. They must justify their proposals using data from the experiment.
5. **Evaluate**: students review their initial ideas, compare what they thought before with what they can now explain, complete a short self-assessment and submit an evidence-based conclusion. The teacher assesses the quality of the experimental design, the use of data and the final explanation.

name: Jigsaw
summary: A cooperative learning technique in which each student works on a part of a topic and then teaches it to their team. It helps to foster positive interdependence, individual responsibility, reading comprehension, and shared synthesis.
desc:
This technique is especially useful for areas of knowledge where the content can be broken down into different parts (for example: literature, social sciences, natural sciences, etc.). In short, the development or implementation of this technique involves the following steps:

- Divide the class into heterogeneous groups of 4 or 5 members each.

- The study material is divided into as many parts as there are members in the group, so that each member receives a fragment of the information on the topic that all the groups are studying together, and does not receive the information that has been made available to their classmates to prepare their own subtopic.

- Each member of the group prepares their part based on the information provided by the teacher or the information they have found themselves. - Next, with the members of the other teams who have studied the same subtopic, they form an "expert group," where they exchange information, delve deeper into key concepts, build diagrams and concept maps, clarify any doubts, etc.; we could say they become experts in their section.

- Then, each of them returns to their original team and is responsible for explaining their prepared part to the group.

Thus, all the students need each other and are "obliged" to cooperate, because each of them only has one piece of the puzzle, and their teammates have the others, essential for successfully completing the proposed task: the overall mastery of a previously fragmented subject of study.

Flippity has a special function for this technique. In the *Random Name Picker* option, once the groups are formed, you will see a puzzle piece. This will create groups made up of one member from each of the initial teams (the expert groups). If we press the same button again, the original groups will reappear.
Formation of groups with 3 members each:

Pressing the button will create the “expert groups,” with one member from each of the previous groups.

---
Source: Pérez, M. (2015). Cooperative Dynamics.

---

**Explanation of the technique:**

## Example

In a unit on the digestive system, the teacher divides the content into four sections: mouth and esophagus, stomach, small intestine and large intestine. In each home team, each student receives a different section and prepares it using a text, a diagram and an image.

Then all students with the same section meet in expert groups to resolve questions, agree on the essential ideas and prepare a clear explanation. After that, they return to their home team and explain their part to the others.

The team completes a general diagram of the digestive process, linking the phases to one another and explaining what happens to food in each one. The task can only be finished if all members contribute their piece correctly.

name: The Sack of Doubts
summary: A structure in which students write down their questions, try to solve them as a team, and then put the unanswered questions in a common container. This helps to identify difficulties, promote mutual support, and guide the teacher's explanation.
desc:
1. Each team member writes a question that has arisen while studying a particular topic on a third of a sheet of paper (including their name and the team name).

2. After a few minutes to allow everyone time to write their question, each member shares it with the rest of their team so that, if anyone can answer it, they can do so. If someone knows the answer, the student who had the question writes it down in their notebook. If no one on the team knows the answer, they give it to the teacher, who adds it to the class's "question bank."

3. In the second part of the session, the teacher draws a question from the "question bank" and asks if anyone from another team knows the answer. If no one knows the answer, the teacher answers the question.

This structure is especially useful for highlighting the interaction (in this case, in the form of solidarity or mutual support) that must exist throughout the entire class, not just within a single team, since different teams help each other resolve doubts that a particular team has been unable to answer.

Source: Pujolàs, P. & Lago, J. R. (2011). The CA/AC program (“cooperate to learn/learn to cooperate”) for teaching team learning: Implementing cooperative learning in the classroom.

## Example

After a lesson on linear equations, each student writes down a specific question: “Why does a term change sign when it moves to the other side?”, “What should I do if there are parentheses?” or “How can I check whether the solution is correct?”.

First, the team tries to answer its members’ questions. Questions they cannot answer are placed in the class question bank. The teacher draws some of them and asks whether another team can explain them. At the end, the most repeated doubts are solved collectively, and the teacher identifies which aspects need further explanation.

name: Traffic Light
summary: A three-level self-assessment routine: what is understood, what is doubtful, and what is not understood. It serves to highlight difficulties, regulate learning, guide clarifications, and encourage reflection on understanding.
desc:
This routine allows for critical reflection on what has been understood from an explanation or topic. It is done using three columns that can be filled out individually or in pairs:

- **Green**: What have I understood?

- **Yellow**: What do I have questions about?

- **Red**: What haven't I understood?

You can use a template similar to this:

## Example

At the end of an explanation about photosynthesis, each student completes three columns. In green, they write: “I understand that plants make organic matter using light, water and carbon dioxide”. In yellow, they write: “I still have questions about the role of mineral salts”. In red, they may write: “I do not fully understand what happens in the chloroplasts”.

The teacher collects the responses or reviews them visually and spends the first minutes of the next lesson clarifying the most frequent yellow and red doubts. The routine helps adjust teaching before moving on to new content.

name: Team-Pair-Solo
summary: This technique involves gradually withdrawing support: first, the problem is solved as a team, then in pairs, and finally individually. It helps develop autonomy, consolidate procedures, and improve confidence when facing similar problems.
desc:
This technique is based on the gradual withdrawal of support.

1. Students receive three problems of similar difficulty.

1. The first problem is solved in teams of four.

2. The second problem is solved in teams of two.

3. The last problem is solved individually.

4. Finally, the results are compared with the rest of the group.

If necessary in steps 3 and 4, students can ask for help from the other pair or their partner, respectively.

Source: *Team-Pair-Solo, Cuseu*, 2002 in Grup de recerca sobre aprenentatge entre iguals. (2016).

## Example

In a mathematics lesson, the teacher proposes three similar problems about percentages. The first is solved in teams of four: calculating the final price of a product with a discount and VAT. The second is solved in pairs: comparing two offers with different discounts. The third is solved individually: deciding which purchase option is cheaper and justifying the answer.

At the end, the team compares the procedures used and reviews mistakes. The structure allows students to practise first with broad support, then with reduced support and finally independently.

name: Listener Teams
summary: This structure involves teams taking on listening roles during a presentation, such as asking questions, agreeing, disagreeing, or giving examples. It helps to increase attention, process information, and generate active participation during explanations.
desc:
Small Grouping

Objectives

- To promote attention and concentration.

- To foster engagement in the development of the content.

- To increase the level of information processing.

## Development

The steps to follow are:

1. Divide the students into four teams and assign them the following tasks.

1. **Questioning**: After the presentation, ask at least two questions about the material covered.

2. **Approve**: After the presentation, indicate which points you agreed with or found useful and why.

3. **Disapprove**: After the presentation, discuss what you disagreed with (or found useless) and explain why.

4. **Give Examples**: After the presentation, provide specific applications or examples of the material.

5. Present the material. Afterward, wait a few moments for the teams to complete their tasks.

1. Ask each group to question, approve, etc.

1. In the next session, the groups exchange roles.

Tips:

- Before starting the class, ask questions that will be answered during the presentation. Ask the students to be alert to find these answers.

- Distribute the different roles among the members of each team. Reach a consensus within the group on the final proposal.

Source: Varas, M. & Zariquiey, F. (2011). Formal and informal techniques of cooperative learning.

## Example

During an explanation about climate change, the teacher divides the class into four listener teams. One team must prepare questions, another must identify ideas they agree with, another must point out possible objections or doubts, and the last one must provide examples from everyday life.

At the end of the presentation, the questioning team asks about causes and consequences, the approval team highlights the usefulness of distinguishing weather from climate, the disagreement team asks about the reliability of some data, and the examples team links the topic to droughts, heat waves or changes in energy consumption. The activity turns a teacher explanation into active and structured listening.

name: Metacognition Ladder
summary: A metacognitive routine that guides students with questions about what they have learned, how, why, and where they can apply it. It serves to promote reflection, transfer of knowledge, self-regulation, and awareness of one's own learning.
desc:
## Description
This routine guides students to reflect on their own learning through a sequence of questions. It allows them to move from identifying what they have learned to transferring that learning to new situations.

## How it Works
1. **What have I learned?** Students identify the knowledge or skills they have worked on.

2. **How ​​did I learn it?** They describe the strategies, resources, or aids they used.

3. **What was it useful for?** Students connect the learning to a specific application.

4. **Where can I apply it?** They propose new situations where they can use what they have learned.

## Objectives
- To foster awareness of one's own learning.

- To improve knowledge transfer.

- To develop self-regulation and reflective thinking.

## Example
After completing a laboratory exercise, students fill in the ladder, indicating what they have learned about experimental design, how they controlled variables, and in what other experiments they could apply that procedure.

name: Learning Stations
summary: Organizing the classroom into stations with different tasks related to the same content helps to diversify activities, cater to different learning paces, promote cooperation, gather varied evidence, and foster student autonomy.
desc:
## Description
Classroom organization into different stations or workspaces. At each station, students perform a different task related to the same content or competency. This allows for combining hands-on, digital, reading, guided practice, and assessment activities.

## Operation
1. The teacher designs several stations with clear tasks and defined timeframes.

2. Students work in small groups and rotate through the stations.

3. Each station can have different materials, instructions, and products.

4. The teacher provides support, answers questions, and collects evidence.

5. At the end, a group discussion or individual reflection takes place.

## Objectives
- To diversify activities and work paces.

- To promote autonomy and cooperation.

- To gather varied evidence of learning.

## Example
In biology, one station involves observing samples, another image analysis, another answering questions, and another creating a diagram. All groups rotate through all the stations.

name: Fishbowl (Aquarium)
summary: A discussion technique in which a small group engages in dialogue while the rest observes with a specific task. It serves to improve the quality of debate, listening, argumentation, and peer evaluation of oral communication.
desc:
## Description
A discussion technique in which a small group engages in dialogue in the center while the rest observes with a specific task. Afterward, the roles are reversed, or a general discussion begins.

## How it works
1. A question, dilemma, or problem is prepared for discussion.

2. A small group sits in the center and converses.

3. The rest observe the quality of the arguments, participation, and main ideas.

4. After a while, some observers enter the "fishbowl" (the central area) and others leave.

5. The session concludes with a summary of ideas and an evaluation of the process.

## Objectives
- Improve the quality of debate.

- Differentiate between participation, listening, and critical observation.

- Encourage peer evaluation of oral communication.

## Example
In social sciences, the central group debates whether a city should limit private vehicle traffic. The observers note arguments based on data, relevant questions, and moments when others are or are not being listened to.

name: Flipped Classroom
summary: This methodology involves covering theoretical explanations outside of class, while class time is dedicated to questions and activities. It fosters autonomy, allows for tailored instruction, and frees up in-person sessions for applying knowledge and solving problems.
desc:
The flipped classroom is a methodology where the teacher's theoretical explanation is transferred from the classroom to the student's home.

In the flipped classroom, the teacher's theoretical explanation is transferred from the classroom to the student's home. This explanation is usually in video format, preferably short to avoid loss of interest.

This frees up some of the time previously dedicated to explanations, which can then be used for other tasks.

When starting out, it is recommended to use the materials already available (documents, presentations, etc.) and gradually develop new videos and materials.

To consolidate this process, questionnaires are used to monitor what the student may or may not have learned on their own.

Sometimes these questionnaires are reviewed before the in-person class to focus the lesson directly on any problematic points that some students may have.

Since the explanations are given outside of school hours, this time is now used for answering questions and completing assignments, things that were previously often done at home.

## Flipped Classroom Methodology

## Graphic Model

Author's own work

## Technological Model for *Flipped Classroom*

Source: Basso-Aránguiz et al. (2018). Proposal of a technological model for Flipped Classroom (T-FliC) in higher education.

The following video, although a bit long, explains in considerable detail what it consists of, both in its pedagogical foundations and its development.

Software Selection for the Flipped Classroom.

## Example

In a mathematics unit on linear functions, the teacher prepares a short 8-minute video with the initial explanation: the concept of function, slope, y-intercept and how to read a graph. Along with the video, students complete a short form with three comprehension questions.

Before class, the teacher reviews the answers and notices that many students confuse slope with initial value. In class, the first few minutes are used to clarify this difficulty, and then students work on a practical activity: interpreting graphs of phone tariffs, journeys and electricity consumption. Face-to-face time is used to answer questions, compare strategies and apply the content to specific situations.

name: Gallery Walk
summary: This technique involves teams presenting their work, while others rotate through to observe and provide feedback. It fosters peer evaluation, constructive feedback, visual communication, and the review of student work.
desc:
## Description
Technique for the presentation and review of group work.

## Operation
1. Teams display their work (posters, maps, etc.) on the classroom walls.

2. Groups rotate through the different stations as if they were in an art gallery.

3. At each station, they discuss each other's work and leave questions or constructive feedback (usually on sticky notes).

4. The authors return to their posters and review the feedback received.

## Objectives
To promote peer assessment and visual communication.

## Example

In a unit on ecosystems, each team creates a poster about a biome: Mediterranean forest, desert, tundra, tropical rainforest or marine ecosystem. The poster must include climate, characteristic species, food relationships and main threats.

The posters are placed on the classroom walls. Groups rotate around the room and leave comments on sticky notes: one question, one well-explained aspect and one suggestion for improvement. When they return to their poster, each team reviews the feedback received and improves the work before submitting or presenting it.

name: Generate Hypotheses
summary: A routine in which students formulate hypotheses about a fact or concept and seek evidence to test them. It helps develop critical thinking, research skills, argumentation, and reflection on the validity of their own ideas.
desc:
The "Generate Hypotheses" thinking routine can be very useful in helping students reflect on the concepts or facts they are studying and test their ideas with data or evidence. This routine can be applied both at the beginning of a topic and during its development, as it can be used as a starting point to begin exploring a new concept or as a tool to deepen the understanding of a concept already studied.

Teacher:

1. Present students with a concept or fact they are studying or that you would like them to investigate.

2. Ask students to formulate hypotheses or conjectures about this concept or fact. It may be helpful to have them reflect on the different points of view or perspectives on the matter.

3. Ask students to write their hypotheses clearly and concisely.

Student:

4. Reflect on the concept or fact you are studying.

5. Formulate one or more hypotheses about this concept or fact. Try to express them clearly and concisely. 1. Look for data or evidence that can help test your hypotheses. It can be helpful to use diverse sources of information or ask your teacher or classmates for help.

2. Analyze the data or evidence you have found and reflect on whether your hypotheses have been confirmed or disproven.

3. Write a summary of your research results and explain whether your hypotheses have been confirmed or disproven.

This can be done individually or in groups, depending on the learning needs and objectives.

If done individually, students can use this routine to reflect on the concepts or facts they are studying and test their ideas with data or evidence. This can help them better understand the concepts and develop critical thinking and reflection skills.

If done in groups, they can use this routine to debate and discuss their ideas with their classmates. This can help them better understand different perspectives and develop communication and teamwork skills. It can also help them enrich their research with the ideas and information of others.

## Example

In a biology lesson, the teacher shows two plants of the same species: one with green leaves and another with yellowish leaves. The teacher asks: “Why is one of the plants showing chlorosis?”.

Students generate hypotheses such as lack of light, overwatering, mineral deficiency or the presence of disease. Then each group decides what data they would need to test its hypothesis: growing conditions, amount of water, soil type, light exposure or presence of insects. Finally, they compare the available evidence and review which hypothesis seems most plausible.

name: Generate Solutions
summary: A routine in which students propose, evaluate, and select solutions to a situation or problem. It helps develop analysis, decision-making, critical thinking, and cooperation in the search for viable answers.
desc:
The "Generate Solutions" thinking routine is a very useful tool to help students develop **analysis and problem-solving** skills. This routine can be used at different points in the learning process, whether at the beginning, during, or at the end of a topic.

Here are the steps to use this routine effectively:

Teacher's role:

1. Present students with a problem or situation to solve. This could be a problem related to the topic you are studying, or a new situation that allows them to apply the knowledge they have acquired.

2. Ask students to generate possible solutions to the problem or situation. You can have them write down the solutions or share them verbally.

3. Ask students to evaluate the different solutions they have generated. You can have them use specific criteria for this evaluation, such as feasibility, practicality, or impact.

4. Ask students to select the best solution from among those they have evaluated.

Students' role:

1. Listen carefully to the problem or situation presented.

2. Generate possible solutions to the problem or situation. You can do this individually or in a group.

3. Evaluate the different solutions you have generated, using the criteria established by the teacher.

4. Select the best solution from those you have evaluated.

## Example

In a digitalization class, the teacher presents a real problem in the group: many students forget deadlines and required materials. First, students generate possible solutions: a shared calendar, automatic reminders, a weekly board, a checklist for each subject or a rotating student responsible for reminders.

Then each group evaluates the proposals using three criteria: ease of use, impact on organization and time needed to maintain it. Finally, they select one solution, justify why they consider it the most appropriate and design an initial version to test for one week.

name: Cooperative Graffiti
summary: A collaborative, written, and rotating brainstorming activity. It serves to generate many contributions in a short time, enrich ideas with those of other groups, and build a collective synthesis.
desc:
## Description
Rotating written brainstorming.

## How it works
1. Each team receives several large sheets of paper, each with a different title or question.

2. Each group writes down their ideas on the topic within a limited time.

3. At the signal, the papers are passed to the next group.

4. The new group reads what the previous group has written and adds new ideas or refines existing ones.

## Objectives
To generate a large number of ideas collaboratively and quickly.

## Example

Before starting a project on improving the school playground, the teacher places four large posters with different questions: “What problems does the playground have?”, “What uses should it allow?”, “What rules would be necessary?” and “What improvements would be easy to implement?”.

Each team starts at one poster and writes its ideas for three minutes. Then the posters rotate. The new team reads what the previous group wrote, adds new ideas and marks with an asterisk the ones they find most interesting. At the end, the class reviews all the posters and selects the most feasible proposals to turn into action steps.

name: Exchange difficulties
summary: This technique involves students formulating difficulties or questions, solving them in teams, and exchanging cards with other teams. It helps to identify comprehension problems, foster cooperation, and practice revising answers.
desc:
After an explanation or a topic previously covered in class, the teacher asks the students to write a question or problem on a card.

1. The students work in teams to find the answers to the questions or problems they have written.

2. The students write the answers on the back of the card.

3. The teams exchange cards with another team.
4. The teams try to solve the questions or problems written on the cards they received.

5. If they find a correct answer, they move on to the next one; if not, they review the process to make the necessary corrections.

Source: CRA Los Olivos. (2017). Cooperative Learning Techniques.

## Example
For example, imagine that you want to carry out an activity about the water cycle using the question-and-answer technique. After the presentation of the topic, the teacher would ask the students to think of a question or problem related to the water cycle and write it on a card. Some examples of difficulties or questions that students could formulate include:

- How does transpiration occur?

- What are the benefits of rain?

- How does evaporation occur?

- What are the consequences of forest fires on the water cycle?

Then, the students would work in teams to find the answers to the questions or problems they formulated. Once they found the answers, they would write them on the back of the card. Finally, the teams would exchange the cards with another team and try to solve the problems or questions written on them. If they found a correct answer, they would move on to the next one; if not, they would review their process to make the necessary corrections.

In this way, with this technique, all students would have had the opportunity to reflect on the topic of the water cycle and to work as a team to find the answers to their difficulties. Furthermore, they would have been able to review and correct their problem-solving processes related to this topic.

name: Role Play
summary: A technique in which students adopt a role or character to deal with a situation. It helps develop empathy, communication, understanding of perspectives, decision-making, and problem-solving in simulated contexts.
desc:
This technique involves having participants take on the role of a specific character and engage in an activity or situation where they must make decisions and solve problems. This technique promotes collaboration and communication among group members, as they must work together to resolve the role-playing challenges. Furthermore, it can also foster reflection and understanding of different topics or concepts, because participants must empathize with a character and understand their motivations and decisions.

An example of the role-playing technique could be the following:

Imagine that the group is studying World War II. Participants can divide themselves into different roles, such as political leaders, military personnel, or ordinary citizens from the various countries involved in the war. The group will then have to resolve a series of problems or dilemmas related to the war, such as what decisions each role would make in certain situations or what the consequences of those decisions would be. Thus, participants will have to work together to solve the role-playing challenges while simultaneously reflecting on the different perspectives and motivations of the various characters.

name: KWL (I know, I want to know, I have learned)
summary: A three-stage routine: what students know, what they want to know, and what they have learned. It serves to activate prior knowledge, formulate questions, guide learning, and review progress at the end.
desc:
## Description
This routine organizes learning into three stages: what students know, what they want to know, and what they have learned. It helps activate prior knowledge, formulate questions, and review progress at the end of the activity or unit.

## How it Works
1. Before starting, students complete the **I Know** column with their prior knowledge.

2. Then, they write the questions they would like to know in the **I Want to Know** column.

3. During the activity or at the end, they complete the **I Have Learned** column with what they have learned.

4. The group reviews which questions have been answered and which remain open.

## Objectives
- Activate prior knowledge.

- Transform curiosity into working questions.

- Promote metacognition and final synthesis.

## Example
Before studying ecosystems, students write what they know about food chains, what questions they have about the relationships between organisms, and, at the end, what they have learned about food webs and ecological balance.

name: Compass Points
summary: A routine that uses cardinal points to analyze an idea: enthusiasm, obstacles, needs, and suggestions. It helps evaluate proposals, organize opinions, identify doubts, and decide on next steps in a structured way.
desc:
This technique is used to introduce a topic, idea, or proposal. It involves using cardinal points to express your opinion.

- E: Enthusiasm. What excites you about this idea or proposal? What advantage does it have?

- O: Obstacle/Concern. What do you see as an obstacle or concern with this idea or proposal? What is its disadvantage?

- N: Need to Know. What else do you need to know or discover about this idea or proposal? What additional information would help you evaluate these ideas or proposals?

- S: Suggestions or Stance for Moving Forward. What is your current suggestion, stance, or opinion regarding this idea or proposal? What should be the next step in evaluating this idea or proposal?

Adapted from: Project Zero. (2020). Compass Points

## Example

The teacher presents the following idea or proposal: "The school is considering implementing a new waste collection system to reduce waste generation and improve resource management. What do you think?"

1. **Excitement**. Students express their enthusiasm for this idea or proposal. Some may say it's a very good idea because it would help reduce the amount of waste generated and improve resource management.

2. **Objections**. They raise the obstacles or concerns they have encountered with this idea or proposal. Some may say it's a very good idea, but that an awareness campaign is needed so everyone knows how the new waste collection system works and commits to following it.

3. **Needs**. They explain what else they would need to know or discover about this idea or proposal to be able to evaluate it more accurately. Some may ask for more information about what types of waste can be recycled and how collection will be carried out, others may ask about the resources needed to implement this new system, or they may ask for examples from other schools that have already implemented a similar waste collection system.

1. **Suggestions**. Students offer their suggestions or positions on how to move forward with this idea or proposal and explain what the next step should be to evaluate it. Some may say that a meeting with all students is necessary to explain the new waste collection system and ask for their commitment to following it. Others may say that a survey is needed to assess the level of knowledge about recycling and how it could be improved.

name: The Box
summary: A team-building activity in which students present personal objects that represent their own characteristics. It helps to foster mutual understanding, build trust, develop empathy, and improve self-expression.
desc:
This technique is also known as the **suitcase**.

## Objective

To identify characteristic personality traits of the students in the class group.

## Procedure

- One day, the teacher comes to class with a personalized, decorated box containing several objects that represent a personality trait: hobbies, skills, flaws, quirks, etc. They take the objects out and show them to the class, explaining what each object represents:

- “This chocolate bar means I really like chocolate, and sweets in general,”

- “I have this screwdriver because I need it to fix things that break at home,”...

- Next, the teacher invites the group to do the same, telling them that one day they will have to bring their own box with “their objects” (three or four, as decided). On that day, they will show the objects to their classmates and explain what each one means to them personally.

Conclusions

Sharing aspects of our personality fosters mutual trust and develops empathy.

Source: ECO. (2007). Group Dynamics.

## Example

At the beginning of the school year, the teacher brings a box with four objects: a notebook, a small plant, a USB drive and a bicycle part. The teacher explains that the notebook represents a liking for writing and organizing ideas, the plant an interest in biology, the USB drive a connection with technology and the bicycle part a personal hobby.

Then each student prepares their own box with three objects that explain something about their personality, interests or skills. In the following session, they present the objects to the group and answer a question from their classmates. The activity allows students to get to know one another personally without focusing on physical appearance.

name: Interview
summary: This technique involves students interviewing each other in pairs or small groups and then presenting a summary about their partner. It helps improve interpersonal skills, listening, communication, and group cohesion.
desc:
This technique allows for a deeper understanding among classmates.

1. Students form pairs or groups of three randomly (see, for example, Sporadic Groups with Flippity).

1. First, they agree on an interview about their skills, aptitudes, and weaknesses to get to know each other better.

1. Then, taking turns, they interview each other.

1. In the second phase, each student writes a sentence summarizing their partner.

1. Finally, each student reads their sentence to the rest of the group.

Adapted from Pujolàs, P. & Lago, J. R. (2011). The CA/AC program (“cooperate to learn/learn to cooperate”) for teaching team learning: Implementing cooperative learning in the classroom.

## Example

During the first week of cooperative teamwork, the teacher forms random pairs and gives them a short interview guide: “What are you good at?”, “What is harder for you in class?”, “How do you prefer to work?”, “What can you contribute to a team?” and “What do you need from your classmates to work better?”.

After interviewing each other, each student writes a sentence about their partner, for example: “Marta is organized, prefers clear tasks and can help the team distribute the work”. At the end, the sentences are read aloud and help build a richer picture of the group.

name: Round Table
summary: A group dialogue technique focused on a question or problem. It serves to promote participation, listen to different perspectives, build consensus, improve communication, and seek shared solutions.
desc:
The round table is a group work technique based on the active participation of all group members. It is conducted by gathering participants around a table so that everyone can see and hear each other. A question is then posed or a problem is presented to the group, and each person has the opportunity to give their opinion or contribute.

The purpose of the round table is to promote communication and dialogue within the group, so that everyone can express their ideas and opinions respectfully. Furthermore, participants work collaboratively to reach an agreement or a common solution that takes into account the ideas and perspectives of all group members.

The round table is a useful technique for improving participants' communication and negotiation skills, as they must be able to express their ideas clearly and concisely and be willing to listen to and consider the ideas of others. It can also be useful for improving conflict resolution within the group because it promotes discussion and dialogue to reach an agreement or a solution that is satisfactory for everyone.

To carry it out, we can do the following:

1. Establish a list of questions or problems you want to discuss in the round table.

2. Invite the participants to sit around the table so that everyone can see and hear each other.

3. Begin the round table by asking the first question or presenting the first problem.

4. Ask each person to express their opinion or make a contribution regarding the question or problem.

5. Encourage dialogue and group discussion, but remember that it is important for everyone to have the opportunity to speak.

6. Listen carefully to the ideas and opinions of others, and try to consider their perspectives.

7. Work collaboratively to reach an agreement or a common solution that takes into account the ideas and perspectives of all group members.

1. Finally, summarize the agreements and solutions reached in the roundtable discussion, and assign responsibilities for their implementation.

## Example

In a tutoring session, the group holds a round table on the question: “How can we improve mobile phone use during lessons and breaks?”. Each student has a short turn to express an opinion, share an experience or propose a rule.

The teacher moderates so that no one dominates the conversation and writes down the most repeated proposals. Then the group agrees on three specific measures, such as leaving phones in a common area during certain activities, allowing their use only when the task requires it, and reviewing the agreement after two weeks. The technique allows students to dialogue, negotiate and reach a shared decision.

name: Pencils in the Middle
summary: A cooperative technique in which pencils are left in the center while an answer is discussed before being written down. It helps to promote equitable participation, argumentation, consensus, and collaborative task completion.
desc:
TEAM SIZE: Base teams.

OBJECTIVES: To solve problems, review what they have learned, debate and argue, share and reach consensus on opinions.

STEPS:

1. Everyone's pencils are placed in the center of the table: only speaking and listening are allowed, not writing.

2. The teacher gives each team a sheet with as many questions or exercises as there are members in the group.

3. Each member will be responsible for one of the questions; that is, they will be in charge of reading it aloud and coordinating the contributions of the other team members when it comes to providing a possible answer.

4. Each member of the team must give an answer, arguing their reasons. The group then debates and decides which is the correct answer. 1. When a consensus is reached, we proceed to take the pencil that was in the center of the table (hence the name of the technique) and carry out the exercise (all at the same time, which guarantees simultaneity).

1. Once the first exercise is solved, it will be the turn of the person to the right, who will become responsible for leading the solution of the second exercise, and so on.

OBSERVATIONS AND PRACTICAL TIPS:

This can be combined with the "Equal Numbers Together" structure.

It is very useful for promoting the equal participation of each and every member, since everyone must participate as coordinators of the process and must also contribute to producing answers for each and every question posed (equal participation and simultaneous interaction).

It will be very important to keep in mind and apply here one of the basic principles governing cooperative teamwork: the economy of resources. This concept refers to the fact that we don't give each student a sheet with the questions; instead, we provide only one sheet per base team. This significantly improves the active listening process, as students must pay attention to the coordinator's answer for each question; otherwise, they won't be able to successfully complete the task. Furthermore, it prevents some students from rushing ahead of the rest of the team in completing tasks.

Source: Pérez, M. (2015). Cooperative Dynamics.

## Example

In biology, each team receives a sheet with four questions about nutrition: the function of carbohydrates, the difference between food and nutrient, the role of vitamins and the consequences of an unbalanced diet. The pencils remain in the middle while each question is discussed.

The student responsible for the first question reads it and coordinates the contributions. Everyone must contribute an idea before writing. When the team reaches a shared answer, everyone takes their pencil and writes it in their notebook. Then the turn passes to the next classmate. This prevents one student from solving the whole task alone and promotes peer explanation.

name: Shared Reading
summary: A cooperative reading technique in which students take turns reading, explaining, and evaluating excerpts from a text. It helps improve reading comprehension, active listening, oral expression, and the shared construction of meaning.
desc:
Excerpt from Santiago Moll's article, "Cooperative Learning: Shared Reading":

The activity consists of four rounds of three steps each.

1. The class is divided into groups of four students (student 1, student 2, student 3, student 4).

1. Student 1 reads a paragraph, text, or comment aloud to students 2, 3, and 4.

1. Students 2, 3, and 4 must actively listen to what student 1 is reading.

1. Once student 1 has finished reading, they pass the baton to student 2, who will be responsible for explaining, commenting on, or summarizing what student 1 has read. This can be done clockwise.

1. Students 3 and 4, who have also listened attentively, will decide whether what student 2 explained, commented on, or summarized is correct. If they disagree, they will give their personal opinion and present it for later evaluation. Students 2 and 3 can also give their opinion after listening to students 3 and 4.

1. Student 2 is now in charge of reading the following excerpt or text, and student 3 is in charge of explaining it.

1. Students 4 and 1 decide whether it is well explained or not. Students 2 and 3 can also give their opinion after what students 4 and 1 have said.

1. The process is repeated until all students have read, explained, and evaluated each part of the text.

---

Explanation of the technique (simplified version):

## Example

In a social sciences lesson, the group reads a short text about the causes of the French Revolution. In teams of four, the first student reads the first paragraph; the second summarizes it in their own words; the third and fourth check whether the summary includes the essential ideas and add clarifications.

Then the second student reads the next paragraph and the third summarizes it. The process continues until the text is finished. At the end, each team creates a shared list of the economic, social and political causes they have identified. The technique helps maintain attention and check comprehension during reading, not only at the end.

name: Ghost Reading
summary: A collective oral reading technique in which students spontaneously continue reading when another student stops. It helps improve comprehension, reading fluency, oral expression, attention, and respect within the group.
desc:
Ghost reading is a technique that can be used with any type of text and aims to improve students' reading comprehension and oral expression. Furthermore, this technique fosters active listening and respect among group members.

It involves reading a text together; when one person finishes, another begins voluntarily.

1. The first reader (which could be the teacher) begins reading a text.

2. At a certain point, they stop reading, and another student begins spontaneously (more than one person may start at the same time, or none may).

3. When they feel ready, this student also stops reading, and another student begins. In this way, everyone reads the text together.

This technique is worth practicing more than once, as skills improve significantly over time.

## Example

In a language lesson, the teacher begins reading a short story aloud. After a few lines, the teacher stops at a natural point in the reading. A student continues voluntarily and, when they think it is appropriate, stops so that another classmate can continue.

At the end, the group discusses whether the rhythm was maintained, whether the pauses helped them understand the text and which parts were more difficult. In later sessions, students improve fluency, lose fear of reading aloud and learn to stay attentive so they can join the reading at the right moment.

name: What I know and what we know
summary: This activity involves participants first writing down what they know about a topic individually, and then collaboratively developing a response. It serves to activate prior knowledge, compare ideas, and guide the development of the session.
desc:
This activity aims to guide students toward the content and develop creativity.

1. The teacher announces the topic that will be covered during the session and asks the students to write a few lines about what it suggests to them. To do this, the teacher gives each student a worksheet. The worksheet has two sections: individual ("What I know...") and collaborative ("What we know...").

1. Individually, the students write what they know about the topic in the first section.

2. Once they have compiled their ideas, the students pair up with the classmate closest to them and create a text that incorporates both of their contributions. Both students write the text in the second section and sign both worksheets. They give one to the teacher and keep the other for the group discussion.

3. A group discussion is held. Afterward, the second worksheet is handed in.

TIPS

- Establish and monitor the time allotted for each phase.

- Value the generation of responses even more than their accuracy.

- Utilize student contributions when developing the topic.

Source: CRA Los Olivos. (2017). Cooperative learning techniques.

## Example

Before starting a unit on ecosystems, the teacher gives students a worksheet with two sections: “What I know” and “What we know”. Individually, each student writes ideas such as “there are producers and consumers”, “plants carry out photosynthesis” or “animals depend on one another”.

Then students form pairs and build a shared text using both contributions, correcting errors and adding new relationships. In the whole-class discussion, the teacher collects the most repeated contributions and uses them to start the unit, identify prior ideas and formulate working questions.

name: 5 Whys
summary: A routine based on chaining together "why" questions to delve deeper into a concept or problem. It helps improve understanding, identify causes, connect ideas, and develop critical thinking.
desc:
The "5 Whys" technique can be used to delve deeper into the concepts and ideas being studied and to help students better understand different topics. For example, consecutive questions such as "Why is this concept important?" or "Why is this concept related to others?" can be asked to help students reflect on the different concepts and their relationships.

This can be done by using consecutive questions (initially five, but this is not a strict rule; ask as many as necessary) that help students reflect on the different concepts and their relationships.

To use the "5 Whys" technique, teachers can follow these steps:

1. Select the topic or concept to be studied.

1. Ask an open-ended question about the topic, such as "Why is this concept important?"

1. Encourage students to ask further questions about the topic, using the question "Why?" to delve deeper into the different concepts.

1. Guide students to ask relevant questions that help them better understand the concepts and to avoid giving closed or direct answers.

1. Record the questions and answers in a list or a concept map to help students visualize the relationships between the different concepts.

1. Use different strategies to stimulate reflection and discussion among students, such as role-playing or debates.

1. Once students have learned to use the technique, they may find it helpful to use this strategy to reflect on other topics or subjects of study to improve their understanding and learning.

## Example

In a biology lesson, the teacher presents the problem: “A plant in the classroom is wilting”. The group applies the 5 Whys:

1. Why is it wilting? Because its leaves are losing turgor.
2. Why are they losing turgor? Because the cells do not have enough water.
3. Why do they not have enough water? Because the plant is not absorbing it or is losing too much.
4. Why might it not be absorbing water? Because the soil is dry or the roots are damaged.
5. Why is the soil dry? Because it has not been watered regularly or it is near a heat source.

Based on this chain of questions, students propose checking soil moisture, reviewing the plant’s location and setting up a watering plan. The technique helps move from a superficial explanation to more specific and testable causes.

name: Four Wise Students
summary: This technique involves a group of expert students teaching different aspects of a topic to the rest of the group. It promotes peer teaching, responsibility, communication, and cooperative learning.
desc:
This is a simplified version of the jigsaw puzzle technique.

1. The teacher selects four participants from the group who are experts in a particular subject, skill, or procedure (who are "wise" in a specific area). They are asked to prepare well, as they will have to teach their knowledge to the rest of the group.

2. A session is organized one day, during which the students (except those playing the role of "wise") are divided into teams of four members each.

3. In the first phase of the session, each member of each team must approach one of the "four wise" individuals so that, along with the members of the other teams who have approached the same "wise" individual, they can learn or be taught what they know.

1. Then, in the second phase of the session, each student returns to their original team, and each one will have to explain or teach the rest of their teammates what their respective "experts" have taught them. In this way, within each base team, they exchange what each individual has learned from their corresponding "expert."

Source: Pujolàs, P. & Lago, J. R. (2011). The CA/AC program (“cooperate to learn/learn to cooperate”) for teaching team learning: Implementing cooperative learning in the classroom.

## Example
For example, imagine that you want to carry out an activity on the use of weak pronouns in Catalan. You could select four students who consider themselves to have already mastered this topic and ask them to prepare to explain it to the others. Next, the group would be divided into teams of four students, and one student from each team would visit one of the "wise men" to receive instruction on the use of weak pronouns. After this initial phase, each student would return to their team and explain to their classmates what they had learned from the "wise man" they visited. In this way, all students would have learned about the use of weak pronouns thanks to the explanations provided by the different "wise men."

name: Group Investigation
summary: This refers to a working method based on research groups or problem-solving situations. It serves to organize learning around problems, cooperation, and shared research.
desc:
See the section Problem-based learning (PBL).

## Example

In a unit on water pollution, the teacher presents a problematic situation: “Dead fish have appeared in a nearby river, and the local council wants to know what may be happening”. The class is organized into investigation groups, and each group takes on a line of inquiry: possible pollutants, land use near the river, effects on living organisms, environmental regulations and intervention proposals.

Each group searches for information, selects evidence and prepares a partial explanation. Then all groups share their results and write a joint report with hypotheses about the causes, data that should be collected and preventive measures. The activity allows students to investigate a complex problem from complementary perspectives.

name: Four-Part Concept Map
summary: A technique in which a team collaboratively constructs a final concept map or outline. It serves to organize information, synthesize content, distribute responsibilities, and create shared study materials.
desc:
At the end of a topic, as a final synthesis, each team can create a concept map or diagram summarizing everything covered in class on that topic.

1. The teacher will guide the students in deciding together which sections should be included in the map or diagram.

2. Within each base team, the different parts of the map or diagram will be divided among the team members, so that each student will have to come prepared at home (or work in class individually or in pairs) for their assigned part.

3. Then, they will share the parts they have prepared, review the coherence of the resulting map or diagram, and, if necessary, refine it before finalizing it and making a copy for each student, which will serve as study material.

Source: Pujolàs, P. & Lago, J. R. (2011). The CA/AC (“cooperate to learn/learn to cooperate”) program for teaching teamwork and implementing cooperative learning in the classroom.

---

**Explanation of the technique:**

## Example

At the end of a unit on the nervous system, the teacher agrees with the class on the main sections of the concept map: neurons, central nervous system, peripheral nervous system, reflex actions and coordination.

In each team, one student prepares the part on neurons, another the central nervous system, another the peripheral nervous system and another reflex actions. Then they share their parts and build a single concept map with clear connectors: “transmits”, “coordinates”, “receives information”, “responds to stimuli”. Before finishing it, they check whether the relationships are correct and whether any important concept is missing.

name: Problem Matrix
summary: A technique that organizes possible solutions and consequences in a table or matrix. It is used to analyze alternatives, compare effects, make informed decisions, and work on problem-solving in an organized way.
desc:
The "problem matrix" technique can be used to help students identify different possible solutions to a problem and evaluate which options are best based on their potential effects or consequences. A table can be created with different possible solutions to a problem in one column and the potential effects or consequences of these solutions in another column. Students can then compare the different options to decide which is the best solution.

The following steps can be followed:

1. Identify the problem or issue to be solved.

2. List possible solutions to the problem.

3. List the potential effects or consequences of each solution.

4. Organize the solutions and consequences in a table or matrix.

5. Compare the different options to determine the best solution based on the potential effects or consequences.
6. Justify the decision made with arguments and examples.

7. Track how the chosen solution has been implemented and evaluate the results obtained.

Example

This table summarizes ideas related to "how to improve academic performance":

| Solution | Effect or consequence |

---|---|

|Organize time better |Improves time management and concentration|

|Study in groups |Improves comprehension and problem-solving skills|

|Get enough rest |Improves concentration and memory|

|Eat healthily |Improves concentration and memory|

|Set goals |Improves motivation and concentration|

|Eliminate distractions |Improves concentration|

This matrix allows you to compare different solutions and assess which are the best options based on their potential effects or consequences.

name: I like it / I don't like it
summary: This activity involves students physically positioning themselves according to their tastes, preferences, or opinions. It helps foster mutual understanding, express shared interests, accept differences, and strengthen group cohesion.
desc:
This technique allows for group understanding.

Draw an imaginary line in the classroom and ask participants to stand on one side of it (let's say, the left). The facilitator will ask those who enjoy something (for example, dancing) to stand on the other side. They will have to suggest things they might like or dislike (some for most and others for only a few), for example, singing, going to the dentist, etc.

In this way, sometimes some will be with the majority of the group, and other times they won't.

Once this is done, invite the participants who agree with the facilitator to also move to that side, and offer those who partially agree the possibility of approaching the line, but without crossing it.

Afterward, we share and discuss the activity:

- How do I feel expressing my feelings?

- Can we sometimes see that other people feel the same way we do?

- How does it make us feel when we see that other people feel the same way?

Sources: 

- Dipsalut. (2016). Dynamics of group cohesion and acceptance.
- Group dynamics. (2021). Dynamic Likes and Dislikes.

## Example

In a tutoring session, the teacher draws an imaginary line in the classroom. The teacher says statements such as: “I like working in groups”, “I like speaking in public”, “I find it hard to ask for help”, “I like creative activities” or “I prefer tasks with very clear instructions”.

Students move to one side or the other depending on whether they agree or disagree; those who partly agree stay near the line. Then the group discusses what they have discovered: some people share unexpected preferences, and others see that they are not the only ones with certain difficulties. The activity helps students get to know the group and normalize differences.

name: Minute Paper
summary: A brief formative assessment technique in which students answer one or two questions at the end of an activity. It serves to detect understanding, identify doubts, and adjust the next session.
desc:
## Description
A very brief formative assessment technique that allows for the quick collection of information about what students have understood and any remaining questions. It can be used at the end of an explanation, an activity, or a session.

## How it works
1. The teacher sets aside one or two minutes at the end of the class.

2. Students answer one or two questions in writing.

3. The teacher reviews the answers to adjust the next session.

4. If appropriate, some answers are discussed anonymously.

## Possible questions
- What important idea did you learn today?

- What point was least clear to you?

- What example could you give of what we covered?

- What question would you ask to check if someone has understood?

## Example
After studying mitosis, students write on a card the most important difference between mitosis and meiosis and one question they still have.

name: Muddiest Point
summary: Formative assessment technique focused on identifying the most confusing point in an explanation or activity. It serves to highlight difficulties, prepare clarifications, and adjust instruction to real needs.
desc:
## Description
Formative assessment technique focused on identifying the aspect that students found most difficult or confusing. It is useful for adjusting explanations, preparing reinforcement activities, or detecting conceptual errors.

## How it works
1. At the end of an explanation or activity, the teacher asks: **What was the most confusing part of today's lesson?**
2. Each student answers briefly and concisely.

3. The teacher groups the questions by topic or type of difficulty.

4. In the next session, the most frequently asked questions are clarified.

## Objectives
- To make students' real difficulties visible.

- To promote self-regulated learning.

- To adjust teaching based on quick observations.

## Example
After explaining exponential functions, several students indicate that the most confusing point was interpreting the meaning of the base. The teacher prepares a short activity with comparative graphs.

name: Numbered Heads Together
summary: A cooperative structure in which all members prepare a task and one is chosen at random to respond. It serves to reinforce individual responsibility, mutual support, review, and formative assessment.
desc:
TEAM SIZE: Base teams.

OBJECTIVES: To activate prior knowledge, answer questions, complete activities and problems, promote peer support and assistance, review what they learn, share and reach consensus on opinions, and assess learning.

STEPS:

1. The teacher assigns a task to the teams, and the team members decide how to solve it, carry it out, and ensure that everyone knows how to do it.

2. After the allotted time has elapsed, the teacher randomly chooses a number from 1 to 4. The students with that number in each team must come to the front of the class to perform the task.

3. Those who successfully complete the task receive some type of reward (praise, applause, a point for their team, etc.).

OBSERVATIONS AND PRACTICAL TIPS:

The task can be completed orally or in writing. Unlike the "number" structure, in this one all teams have the opportunity to earn a reward.

Source: Pérez, M. (2015). Cooperative Dynamics.

---

**Explanation of the technique:**

## Example

In mathematics, the teacher gives each team four proportionality exercises. Each student has a number from 1 to 4. The team solves the exercises and makes sure everyone understands the procedure: identifying quantities, checking whether the relationship is direct or inverse, and justifying the calculation.

When time is up, the teacher calls a random number, for example 3. All students with that number answer on behalf of their teams. Since they do not know who will be chosen, the team must help all members understand the task, not just get a quick answer.

name: Word-Phrase-Sentence
summary: A routine in which students select a significant word, idea, and phrase from a text. It helps them grasp the essence, share interpretations, identify common themes, and improve reading comprehension.
desc:
A cooperative routine for capturing the essence and exploring the meaning of a text from a variety of perspectives.

## Purpose: What kind of thinking does this routine encourage?
This routine is used to capture the essence of a reading and also to identify themes.

## Application: Where and when can we use this routine?
It is best used with short texts, such as a chapter or article. The potential of this routine lies in justifying, in writing or through discussion, the key points. Through subsequent discussion or sharing, either in small groups or as a class, students will begin to identify themes, implications, and predictions that lead to a deeper understanding of the text.

## Method: How can we use this routine?
Students are given the following instructions:

1. Individually, review the text you have read and select:

(5 minutes):

1. A word that caught your attention.

1. An idea (it can be a combination of words) that has provoked you, engaged you, or is meaningful to you.

1. A sentence that has helped you understand the text.

1. In a group, share the word, idea, and sentence you have written and explain why you chose them. One member of the group writes down what the others say.

(10 minutes)

1. Look at the group's responses. Consider:

(15 minutes)

1. What themes are common?

1. Based on the group's responses, what implications, interpretations, or predictions can you draw from the text?

1. What aspects of the text have been overlooked in the group discussion? What leads you to think this?

1. Each member of the group briefly reflects on their new understanding and how the discussion has contributed to it.

(5 minutes)

| ***WORD*** | ***IDEA*** | ***SENTENCE*** |

|*What word caught your attention?* |*What idea is significant to you?* |*What flash of insight helped you better understand the presentation/text?* |

***THEMES**:

What themes are common?

*

---
***IMPLICATIONS**:

What implications, interpretations, or predictions can you draw from the text?

*

---
***OVERLOOKED ASPECTS**:

What aspects of the text were overlooked in the group discussion? What leads you to think this?*

---

## Example

After reading a short article about the use of artificial intelligence in education, each student selects a word, an idea and a sentence. A word might be “personalization”; an idea, “AI can help adapt activities, but it does not replace teacher judgment”; and a sentence, “technology expands possibilities when it is integrated with pedagogical intention”.

In small groups, students share their choices and justify why they selected them. Then they look for common themes, such as autonomy, assessment, personalized support or risks of dependence. The routine helps move from individual reading to shared understanding of the text.

name: Three-Minute Pause
summary: This structure involves the teacher interrupting the explanation to allow teams to formulate questions or summaries. It serves to check understanding, stimulate reflection, and adjust instruction during the session.
desc:
Base Teams

OBJECTIVES: To assess understanding of the material covered, to share and reach consensus on opinions, and to develop summaries.

STEPS:
